questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
A piece of plumber's solder (containing Pb & Sn only) weighing 3 gm was dissolved in dil HNO3 and then treated with dilute H2SO4 - the ppt. of PbSO4 is produced , which after washing and drying weighed 2.93 gm. The remaining solution was neutralized - a white ppt of stannic acid is produced , which on heating yields 1.27 gm of SnO2. Therefore mass % of Pb and Sn in the plumber's solder is

At wt. Pb=207 ; Sn=118.7
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>66.67</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>33.33</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>33.33</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>66.67</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>75</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>25</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math>
questions.solution:
wt of Pb=Wt. of PbWt. of PbSO4×2.93wt of Pb=207303 × 2.93=2 gm
if sample weight is 3 gm than

wt of Sn=1 gm.

∴% Pb=23 × 100=66.67 %; hence % Sn

=100 – 66.67=33.33 %
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A crystalline hydrated salt on being heated loses 45.6% of its weight. Percentage composition of anhydrous salt is Al - 10.5 %, K - 15.1 %, S - 24.8 %, O - 49.6 %. Find molecular formula of salt (hydrated) (M = 948 g/mol for hydrated salt).
questions.solution:
For anhydrous salt

Element Al K S O Atomic ratio 1 0 · 5 2 7 1 5 · 1 3 9 2 4 · 8 3 2 4 9 · 6 1 6 0 · 3 9 0 · 3 9 0 · 7 8 3 · 1 0

Empirical formula of anhydrous salt = KAlS2O8

E.F mass of Anhydrous salt = 258 g/mol

Mass of Moisture = 2 5 8 × 4 5 · 6 5 4 · 4 = 2 1 6  g

= 2 1 6 1 8 ≈ 1 2  mole

Empirical formula of Anhydrous salt KAlS2O8.12H2O

E F  Mass = 2 5 8 + 2 1 6 = 4 7 4  g/Empirical formula mass

n = Mole Mass E.F. Mass = 9 4 8 4 7 4 = 2

Molecular Formula = K2Al2S4O16.24H2O
 
                            = K2SO4.Al2(SO4)3.24H2O
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
H 2 O 2 + 2KI → 40% yield I 2 + 2 KOH H 2 O 2 + 2 KMnO 4 + 3 H 2 SO 4 → 50% yield K 2 SO 4 + 2 MnSO 4 + 3 O 2 + 4 H 2 O

150 mL of H2O2 sample was divided into two parts. First part was treated with KI and formed KOH required 200 mL of M/2 H2SO4 for neutralisation. Other part was treated with KMnO4 yielding 6.74 litre of O2 at 1 atm and 273 K. Using % yield indicated, the volume strength of H2O2 sample used is:
questions.solution:
2KOH + H2SO4  →K2SO4 + 2H2O

2 mole of KOH react with 1 mole of H2SO4.

Mole of H2SO4 = 0.1 ; mole of KOH = 0.2
whereas in first reaction 1 mole H2O2 form 2 mole KOH.
mole of H2O2 used in first reaction

= 0.2 2 × 1 0.4 = 0.25 mole

in second reaction.
mole of produced O 2 = 6.74 22.4 = 0.3  mole

∴   mole of H2O2 used in second reaction

= 0.3 3 × 0.5 = 0.2 mole

Total mole of consumed H2O2 = 0.25 + 0.2 =0.45 mole

Molarity of H 2 O 2 = 0.45 0.15 = 3 M

Volume strength = 11.2 × 3 = 33.6 volumes
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
RH2 (ion exchange resin) can replace Ca2+ ions in hard water as:

RH2 + Ca2+⟶ RCa + 2H+

If 1 L of hard water after passing through RH2 has pH = 3, then hardness in parts per million of Ca2+ is :
questions.solution:
[ H+ ] =10 - 3 M [ ∵ pH = 3 = - log [ H+ ] 

         =10 - 3 moles per litre

[ Ca2 + ] = 5 × 10 - 4 moles per litre

         = 5 × 10 - 1 moles of Ca2 + per 103 L

         = 5 × 10 - 1× 40 g Ca2 + per 106 g of H2O

Ca2 + = 20 ppm = (H).
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: A metal <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> forms the sulphate <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> A <math><mn>0.596</mn></math> gram sample of the sulphate reacts with excess <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> to give <math><mn>1.220</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> What is the atomic weight of <math> <mtext>M</mtext>  </math>(in g/mol)?
questions.solution:
Mole of BaSO4=1.22233.3
Moles of M2SO43=1.22233.3×13
=1.743×10-3
Wt. of M2SO43=0.596
∴  1.743×10-3 2M+96×3=0.596
M=26.9
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Urea is manufactured on large scale by passing CO<sub>2</sub> (g) through ammonia solution followed by crystallization. CO<sub>2</sub> for the above reaction is prepared by combustion of hydrocarbons. If combustion of 236.1 kg of a saturated hydrocarbon produces as much CO<sub>2</sub> as required for production of 1000 kg of urea, deduce the number of carbon atoms in the hydrocarbon.
questions.solution:
Urea manufacture

CO2  + 2 NH3  ⟶    H2 NCO NH2  +  H2 O
44 g                                   60 g

For 1000 Kg urea CO2  required is

4 4 6 0 × 1 0 0 0 × 1 0 3 = 7 3 3 . 3 3 g × 1 0 3

C n H 2 n + 2 + 3n+12 O 2 → n  CO 2 + n + 1  H 2 O  ℓ

n = 7 3 3 . 3 3 4 4 × 1 0 3   moles

Apply POAC to C

n   2 3 6 . 1 × 1 0 3 1 2 n + 2 n + 2 = 7 3 3 . 3 3 4 4 × 1 0 3 × 1

n 1 4 n + 2 × 2 3 6 . 1 = 1 6 . 6 7

236.1n=233.38n+33.34

2.72n=33.34

n = 3 3 . 3 4 2 . 7 2 ≅ 1 2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: The mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math> precipitated when a solution containing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> is added to a solution containing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AgNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is [Atomic mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ag</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>108</mn></math>, Atomic mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Na</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>23</mn></math>]
questions.solution:
NaCl+AgNO3→NaNO3+AgCl58.5 g170143.5 g

(Limiting reagent)  NaCl:AgNO3

Required ratio   58.5:170

Given ratio  11.70:3.4 (Limiting reagent) 

170 g AgNO3→143.5 g AgCl

∴ 3.4 g AgNO3→x

x=3.4×143.5170=2.879 g
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A sample containing HAsO<sub>2</sub> (mol. wt. = 108) and weighing 3.78 g is dissolved and diluted to 250 mL in a volumetric flask. A 50 mL sample (aliquot) is withdrawn with a pipet and titrated with 35 mL of 0.05 M solution of <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>I</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math>. Calculate the percentage HAsO<sub>2</sub> in the sample :
questions.solution:
I 2 + AsO 2 - + 2 H 2 O → HAsO 4 2 - + 2 I - + 3 H +

m-eq. of HAsO 2 (in 50 mL) = m-eq. of I 2

= 3 5 × 0.05 × 2 = 3.5

m-eq. of HAsO 2 in 250 mL = 3.5 × 2 5 0 5 0

= 17.5

wt. of HAsO 2 in sample

= 17.5 2 × 1 0 8 × 1 0 - 3 = 0.945 g

% of HAsO 2 in the sample = 0.945 3.78 × 1 0 0

= 25%
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: $X$ litre of carbon monoxide is present at STP. It is completely oxidized to $\mathrm{CO}_2$. The volume of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ formed is 11.207 litres. What is the value of $X$ in litres?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{CO}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2 \\ & 22.4 \mathrm{~L} \quad 22.4 \mathrm{~L} \\ & x \mathrm{~L} \quad 11.207 \mathrm{~L} \\ & 22.4 \mathrm{~L} \text { of } \mathrm{CO} \text {, oxidized to } \mathrm{CO}_2=22.4 \mathrm{~L} \\ & 1 \mathrm{~L} \text { of } \mathrm{CO} \text {, oxidized to } \mathrm{CO}_2=\frac{224}{224} \mathrm{~L} \\ & \text { 11.202 L of CO, oxidized to } \mathrm{CO}_2 \\ & =\frac{224}{224} \times 11.207=11.207 \mathrm{~L} \\ & \end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.q: <math><mn>25.4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> of iodine and <math><mn>7.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> chlorine are made to react to give a mixture of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math>.The number of moles of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> formed are respectively
questions.solution:
I2+2Cl2⟶ ICl+ICl3

Moles of I2reacted=wt.givenmolecular weight=25.4254=0.1 mol

Moles of Cl2 reacted=7.171=0.1 mol.

Chlorine is the limiting reagent.

Hence

no. of moles of ICl produced=no. of moles of ICl3 produced=no: of moles of productsno: of moles of limiting reagent=0.12=0.05 moles.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following law does this data illustrate: <math><mn>12 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> carbon combines with <math><mn>64</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> sulphur to form <math><mi>C</mi><msub><mrow><mi>S</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo><mn>12</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> carbon also combines with 32 <math><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> oxygen to form <math><mi>C</mi><msub><mrow><mi>O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> sulphur combines with 10 <math><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> oxygen to form <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math>.
questions.solution: Ratio of the weights of S and O combining with fixed weight of C is 64 : 32 = 2 : 1. Ratio of the weights of S and O combining directly=10 :10=1 :1. The two ratios are simple multiple of each other. This proves law of reciprocal proportions.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>63</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of Oxalic acid is dissolved in order to obtain <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup></math> of its solution. Find the normality of this solution. [Oxalic acid: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="" close="]"><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi>COOH</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>·</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
questions.solution:
Molar mass of oxalic acid = 126 g/mol

Mole =Given massMolar mass=0.63126=0.005 mol

Molarity = Mole of soluteVolume of solution in ml×1000=0.005250×1000=0.02M

Equivalent mass of oxalic acid = 1262=63

Gram equivalent of the solute = 0.6363=0.01

Normality=Gram equivalent of soluteVolume of solution in ml×1000=0.01250×1000=0.04N

Hence, normality of solution is 0.04 N.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A compound which contains one atom of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>, two atoms of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> and three atoms of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math>, is made by mixing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>15</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>atoms</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mole</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> atoms. Given that only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of compound results. Calculate the atomic mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> if atomic masses of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi>amu</mi></math>, respectively
questions.solution:
X+2Y+3Z⟶XY2Z3Moles5601·15×10236·023×10230·03⇒0·08330·1910·03MolesCoefficient0·083310·19120·031=0·08330·09500·03

∴ Z is limiting reagent and 0.03 moles of  Z will given 0.01 mole XY2Z3

0 · 0 1  mole ≡ 4 · 4 0  g

       1  mole = 4 4 0  g / mole

           4 4 0 = 6 0 + 2 X  Y + 3 × 8 0

           1 4 0 = 2 y

∴            y = 7 0  amu
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Gaseous benzene reacts with hydrogen gas in the presence of nickel catalyst to give gaseous cyclohexane. A mixture of benzene vapour and hydrogen had a pressure of 60 mm Hg in vessel. After all benzene converted to cyclohexane, the pressure of the gas was 30 mm Hg in the same volume and at the same temperature. What fraction (by mole) of the original mixture was benzene?
questions.solution:
C6H6g+3H2g→C6H12g

Let x and y be the partial pressures (initial) of gaseous benzene and hydrogen in the vessel.

Then, x + y = 6 0    ......... (i)

After the reaction, whole of benzene is converted to cyclohexane.

Partial pressure of cyclohexane =  x mmHg

Partial pressure of hydrogen left unreacted = y - 3 x

Total pressure of the system after the reaction,

x + y - 3 x = y - 2 x = 3 0    .......... (ii)

Solving (i) and (ii), x = 10 mmHg

Number of moles ∝ pressure ; Volume ∝ number of moles

Hence, fraction of benzene by volume in the original mixture = 1 0 6 0 = 1 6 = 0.167
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In a certain operation, 358 g of TiCl4 reacts with 96 g of Mg. Calculate the yield of Ti if 32 g of Ti is actually obtained  [atomic weight of Ti=48, atomic weight of Mg=24]
questions.solution:
Balanced equation :

TiCl4+2Mg→Ti+2MgCl2

 Initial moles of TiCl4 =massmolar mass=358190=1.88

Initial moles of  Mg=9624=4

For finding limiting reagent, moles of both the reactants are divided by their coefficients and the least value gives L.R.

For TiCl4, 1.881=1.88

For Mg = 42=2

∴ TiCl4 is the limiting reagent So, on the basis of L.R., product amount will be calculated.

∵ One mole of TiCl4 produces one mole of Ti.

 Number of moles of Ti produced by 1.88 moles of 

TiCl4=1.88 moles.

Mass of Ti= moles of Ti× molar mass of Ti

=1.88×48=90.24 g

% yield=Experimental yieldTheoretical yield×100

=3290·24×100≅35.46%
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: 4 mol of a mixture of Mohr's salt and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> requires 500 ml of 1 M, <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow></msub></math> for complete oxidation in acidic medium. The mole % of the Mohr's salt in the mixture is
questions.solution:
Cr2O72-+6Fe2+(n=1) (Mohr's salt) +14H+→2Cr3++6Fe3++7H2O

Equivalent of Fe2+= moles of Mohr's salt

= equivalent of K2Cr2O7

=500×10-3×6×1=3

Hence mole percent of Mohr's salt

=34×100=75
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The number of molecules of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ liberated by the complete combustion of $0.1 \mathrm{~g}$ atom of graphite in air is
questions.solution:
$\underset{1\mathrm{~mol}}{\mathrm{C}(s)}+\mathrm{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \underset{1\mathrm{~mol}}{\mathrm{CO}_2(g)}$
$=6.023 \times 10^{23}$
$\because 1$ mole of graphite on complete combustion gives $\mathrm{CO}_2$
$=6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { molecules }$
$\therefore 0.1$ mole of graphite will give $\mathrm{CO}_2$
$=\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23} \times 0.1}{1}$
$=6.023 \times 10^{22}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
10 g of MgCO3 decomposes on heating to 0.1 mole CO2 and 4 g MgO. The percent purity of MgCO3 is
(Given that atomic weights of  Mg, C and O are 24, 12 and 16 u)
questions.solution:
MgC O 3 decomposes as below:

MgC O 3 →MgO+C O 2

mole ratio =1:1:1

Thus 0.1 mole of C O 2 will be produced by 0.1 mole of MgC O 3 .

Mass of MgC O 3 in 10 g sample =0.1×84=8.4 g

Percent purity = 8.4 10 ×100=84%
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo>%</mo></math> pure limestone is heated, the approximate volume (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math>) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> liberated at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>STP</mi></math> is:
questions.solution:
The decomposition reaction of CaCO3 is,

CaCO3 (s)⟶∆CaO(s)+CO2 (g)

At STP, heating 1 mole of CaCO3 (i.e., 100 g) liberates 1 mole or 22.4 L of CO2.

10 g of 90% pure limestone=10×90100

=9 g of pure CaCO3.

Since, 100 g→22.4 L

Therefore, 9 g of CaCO3 will liberate

=9×22.4100L

=2.0 L CO2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: At 100<sup>o</sup>C and 1 atm. if the density of liquid water is <math><mn>1.0 </mn><mtext>gc</mtext><msup><mrow><mtext>m</mtext></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and that of water vapour is <math><mn>0.0006 </mn><mtext>gc</mtext><msup><mrow><mtext>m</mtext></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> , then the volume occupied by water molecules in 1 litre of steam at that temperature
questions.solution:
1L=1000 mL=1000 cm3

mass=density×volume

=(0.0006 g cm-3)×(1000 cm3)=0.6g

As 18 g of water =18 cm3

So 0.6 g of water =0.6 cm3

Hence actual volume occupied by molecules =0.6 cm3
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Based on following information determine the value of x and y
⇒ { CH 3 x AlCl y ⟶ x CH 4 g + ⏟ ↓ y C l - + Al 3 + AgCl s 0 . 6 4 3 g 0 . 2 2 2 g 0 . 9 9 6 9 g I II III

atomic masses of Al,Cl and Ag are respectively 27,35.5,108.
questions.solution:
CH 3 x AlCl y ⟶ x  CH 4 g + ⏟ y  Cl - + Al 3 + ↓ 0 · 6 4 3  g I 0 · 2 2 2  g II AgCl s 0 · 9 9 6 9 III

Cl on PoAC   ⇒

0 · 6 4 3 1 5  x + 2 7 + 3 5 · 5  y × y = 0 · 9 9 6 1 4 3 · 5 × 1         ...(A)

C on PoAC ⇒

0 · 6 4 3 1 5  x + 2 7 + 3 5 · 5  y × x = 0 · 2 2 2 1 6 × 1         ...(B)

by divide B with A A B ⇒ x y = 0·222 16 0·996 143·5 =2

take least ratio in case of salt
∴   x = 2 y = 1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In an experiment, 2.4 g of iron oxide on reduction with hydrogen gave 1.68 g of iron. In another experiment, 2.9 g of iron oxide gave 2.09 g of iron on reduction. Which law is illustrated from the above data?
questions.solution:
In first experiment,

24 g of iron oxide gave 1.68 g of iron.

Mass of iron oxide =2.4 g

Mass of iron =1.68 g

Mass of oxygen =2.4-1.68=0.72

Ratio of masses of iron and oxygen =1.680.72=7:3

In second experiment,

Mass of iron oxide =2.9 g

Mass of iron =2.09 g

Mass of oxygen =2.9-2.09=0.81

Ratio of masses of iron and oxygen =2.090.81=7:3
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
NX is produced by the following step of reactions
M+X2→MX2

3MX2+X2→M3X8

M3X8+N2CO3→NX+CO2+M3O4
How much M (metal) is consumed to produce 206 gm of NX. (Take atomic weight of M=56, N=23, X=80)
questions.solution:
The sequence of reactions are as follows:

M+X2→MX2MX2+X2→M3X8M3X8+Na2CO3→NX+CO2+M3O4

moles of NX=mass of NXmolar mass of NX=206103=2 mol

POAC(Principle of atom conservation) for X Atom :
Number of X atom in M3X8= Number of X Atom in NX
8 [Number of mole M3X8] =1[ of Number of mole of NX]
Number of moles of M3X8  =28=14 mole


Now, POAC for M Atom:

3 [Number of mole of M3X8] =1×[Number of Mole of M]
3×14= Number of mole of M

Weight of M =mol of M×atomic mass of M=34×56=42gram
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: To a 10 ml, 1M aqueous solution of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> , excess of NaOH is added so that all <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is disproportionated to <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><msubsup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo></mrow></msubsup></math> . The resulting solution is made free of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> by extraction and excess of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> is neutralised by acidifying the solution. The resulting solution is sufficient to react with 2g of impure <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math> (M = 128 g/mole) sample. Which of the following is the correct % purity of oxalate sample
questions.solution:
6H++BrO3-+3C2O42-→Br-+6CO2+3H2O
10/3 m mole BrO3- reacts with 10 m. mole C2O42-
Mass of CaC 2 O 4  = 10 ×  10 −3  × 128 = 1.28 g
% purity =  Weight of substance Weight of compound  × 100
% purity = 1.28 2  × 100=64%
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When 20 g of CaCO3  is treated with 20 g HCl, the mass of CO2 formed would be
questions.solution:
So according to the question when the reaction takes place

CaCO3+2HCl→CaCl2+H2O+CO21mol2 mol1 mol100 g73 g44 g

Let CaCO3 be completely consumed in the reaction

So that 

100 g of CaCO3 give 44 g of CO2

Thus,20 g of CaCO3 give

44100 × 20 g = 8.8 g CO2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
NaClO3 is used in spacecraft to produce O2. The daily consumption of pure O2 by a person is 492 L at 1 atm, 300 K. How much amount of NaClO3 (in grams) is required to produce O2 for the daily consumption of a person at 1 atm, 300 K?

NaClO3s+Fes→O2g+NaCls+FeOs  R=0.082 L atm mol–1K–1
questions.solution:
Moles of NaClO3= moles of O2
moles of O2=PVRT=1×4920.082×300=20 moles
As molecular mass of NaClO3=106.5
NaClO3=20×106.5=2130 g
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following gives the molarity of a 17.0% by mass solution of sodium acetate, <math> <mrow> <msub> <mrow> <mtext>CH</mtext></mrow> <mtext>3</mtext> </msub> <mtext>COONa</mtext><mfenced> <mrow> <mtext>FM</mtext><mtext> </mtext><mtext>=</mtext><mtext> </mtext><mtext>82</mtext><mtext>.0</mtext><mtext> </mtext><mtext>amu</mtext></mrow> </mfenced></mrow> </math> in water? Given the density is <math> <mrow> <mtext>1</mtext><mtext>.09</mtext><mtext> </mtext><mtext>g/mol</mtext></mrow> </math>.
questions.solution:
17.0% solution of CH 3 COONa means that 100 g solution contain 17 g of CH 3 COONa .
Moles of CH 3 COONa =  17 g 82.0 g  mol −1  =  17 82.0  mol
Volume of 100 g solution =Mass of solutionDensity of solution
=  100 g 1.09 g  mol −1  =  100 1.09 mL
So molarity of solution =  Moles of solute Liter of soln.
=  17 82.0 mol 100 1.09 ×10 −3  L
= 2.259 M = 2.26 M
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
1 g silver salt of an organic dibasic acid on heating yields 0.5934 g Ag. If the weight percentage of C in acid is 8 times the weight percentage of hydrogen and one half the weight percentage of oxygen, then determine its molecular formula.

MAgNO3=108
questions.solution:
RH2⟶RAg2⟶Ag

Apply principle of atom conservation to Ag,

1.00MRAg2×2=0·534 g108×1

MRAg2=2×1080·5934=364 g mol-1

MRH2=364-216+2=150 g mol-1

Element%CompositionAtomic ratioLeast ratioLeast simple ratioC8812=0.671.002.00H111=1.001.503.00O161616=1.001.503.00

Empirical formula=C2H3O3

Empirical formula mass=75 g per EFM

∴  n=Molecular massEmpirical formula mass=15075=2

The molecular formula is C4H6O6.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
A 1 g sample of KClO3 was heated under such conditions that a part of it decomposed according to the equation.

(i) 2KClO3⟶2KCl+3O2

and the remaining underwent change according to the equation

(ii) 4KClO3⟶3KClO4+KCl

If the amount of O2 evolved was 146.8 mL at NTP, calculate the percentage by weight of KClO4 in the residue.
questions.solution:
Apply POAC on the oxygen atoms in the equation (1)

KClO3→KCl+O2

moles of O- atoms in KClO3= moles of O- atoms in O2 since 1 mole of KClO3 contains 3 moles of O- atoms
and 1 mole of O2 contains 2 moles of O- atoms 3x moles of KClO3=2x moles of O2

3 xwt. of KClO3mol.wt. of KClO3=2 xvolume   of O2 at NTP22400

wt. of  KClO3=2 X 146.8 X 122.53 X 22400=0.5358 g.

In the second reaction:

The amount of KClO3 left =1-0.5358=0.4642g.

KClO3→KClO4+KCl

0.4642g

Applying POAC for O-atoms in the equation (ii),
moles of O- atoms in KClO3= moles of O- atoms inKClO4

since 1 mole of KClO3 contains 3 moles of O- atoms and 1  mole of KClO4 contains 4 moles of O- atoms 3× moles of KClO3=4x moles of KClO4

3xwt. of KClO3mol.wt. of KClO3=4xwt. of KClO4mol.wt. of KClO4

wt. of KClO4=3 x 0.4642 x 138.5122.4 x 4=0.3937 g.

wt. of residue =1- wt. of Oxygen

=1-146.822400×32g=0.7902g

Hence,% of KClO4 in the residue

=0.39370.7902×100=49.8%
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: <math><mn>0.14</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> tablet is added to neutralise <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> of <math><mn>0.1 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> produced in stomach of a man. Then percentage purity of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> tablet is -
questions.solution:
NaHCO3+HCl ⟶NaCl+H2O+CO2

So moles of NaHCO3Pureused=moles of HCl

=0.1 × 5 × 10–3

So weight of pure NaHCO3 used=0.1 × 5 ×

10–3 × 84 grams

=0.042 grams

%Purity=Mass of pure  substanceTotal mass of sample


% Purity of tablet=0.0420.14×100=30%

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: An alloy of metals X and Y weighs <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mi>g</mi><mo>&</mo></math> contains atoms X and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn></math>. The percentage of Metal X in the alloy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> by mass. If the atomic mass of X is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo></math> , what is the atomic mass of metal Y.
questions.solution:
According to mole concept 

Mass of metal X in alloy = 12×20100

Mass of metal Y in alloy = 12-2.4 =9.6 g

Moles of X = 2.440

Now, Number of atoms of X = 2.440×6.023×1023 = 3.61×1022

Number of atoms of  Y = 3.61×1022×52 = 9.025×1022

Thus, by the stoichiometry

9.6g Y →9.025×1022 atom6.022×1023→9.69.025×1022×6.023×1023

Hence, the atomic mass of Y = 64 amu
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The chloride of a metal contains 71% chlorine by weight and the vapour density of it is 50. The atomic weigh of the metal will be:
questions.solution:
Mol. wt. of metal chloride =50×2=100
Let metal chloride be metal chlorine then
Equivalent of metal = Equivalents of chlorine =  W m E m  =  W Cl E Cl  or  29 E  =  71 35.5
E=292
Now a+35.5n=100
or n × E + 35 × 5n = 100
∴n=2
Therefore, a=2×E=2×292=29
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Calculate the density of an aqueous solution of KI, if its molarity and molality are \(1.44 \mathrm{M}\) and \(1.5 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}\) respectively.
questions.solution:
We know,
\(\begin{aligned}
m & =\frac{1000 \times M}{1000 \times d-M \times M_B} \\
\Rightarrow d & =\frac{1000 \times M+m \times M \times M_B}{1000 \times m} \\
= & \frac{1000 \times 1.44+1.5 \times 1.44 \times 166}{1000 \times 1.5} \\
& =1.1990=1.20 \mathrm{gL}^{-1} \\
m & =\text { molality }=1.5 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \\
M & =\text { molarity }=1.44 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \\
M_B & =\text { molar mass of } \mathrm{KI} \\
& =166 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
d & =\text { density of solution }
\end{aligned}\)
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: On subjecting 10 ml mixture of N<sub>2</sub> and CO to repeated electric spark to form CO<sub>2</sub> and NO, 7 ml of O<sub>2</sub> was required for combustion. What was the mole percent of CO in the mixture ? (All volumes were measured under identical conditions)
questions.solution:
N 2 + O 2 → 2 NO CO + 1 2 O 2 → CO 2

Let x ml be the volume of N2 in 10 ml mixture.

Total Volume of O2 required = x + 1 0 - x 2 = 7 (given)

⇒   x = 4 ml

Volume of CO = 1 0 - 4 = 6 ml

Mol % of CO = 6 × 1 0 0 1 0 = 6 0
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: A sample of a hydrate of barium chloride weighing 61 g was heated until all the water of hydration is removed. The dried sample weighed 52 g. The formula of the hydrated salt is: (atomic mass, Ba = 137 amu, Cl = 35.5 amu)
questions.solution:
BaCl2⋅xH2O→BaCl2+x H2O 

(137+2×35.5+18x)

=208+18x  g/mole

208 + 18 x208=6152

10816+936 x=12688

936 x=1872

x=2

Formula is BaCl2⋅2H2O

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
For a given unbalanced reaction, $\mathrm{MnO}_2+\mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MnCl}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$, which is the limiting reagent, if the initial amount for each of the reactant is 100 grams?
[Molar masses : $\mathrm{MnO}_2=86.9 ; \mathrm{HCl}=36.5$; $\left.\mathrm{MnCl}_2=125.8 ; \mathrm{Cl}_2=70.9 ; \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}=18\right]$
questions.solution:
Limiting reagent In a chemical reaction, the limiting reagent is the reactant that determine, how much of the products are made. The other reactants are sometimes referred to as being in excess, since there will be some leftover after the limiting reagent is completely used up. Given, unbalanced reaction, $\begin{array}{lcccc} & \mathrm{MnO}_2+ & \mathrm{HCl} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{MnCl}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\ \text { Initial amount } & 100 \mathrm{~g } & 100 \mathrm{~g} & & \\ \text { of reactant } & & \\ \text { Molar mass } & 86.9 & 36.5 & 125.8 & 18 \end{array}$ Balancing the reaction,
Convert amounts to moles Moles of $\mathrm{MnO}_2=100 \mathrm{~g} \times \frac{1 \mathrm{~mol}}{86.9 \mathrm{~g}}=1.15 \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{MnO}_2$ Moles of $\mathrm{HCl}=100 \mathrm{~g} \times \frac{1 \mathrm{~mol}}{36.5 \mathrm{~g}}=2.73 \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{HCl}$ Actual ratio (molar ratio) of reactants $=\frac{\text { Moles of } \mathrm{MnO}_2}{\text { Moles of } \mathrm{HCl}}=\frac{1.15 \mathrm{~mol}}{2.73 \mathrm{~mol}}=\frac{0.42}{1} \mathrm{~mol}$ The actual ratio tells us that we have 0.42 moles of $\mathrm{MnO}_2$ for every 1 mole of $\mathrm{HCl}$. In comparison, the stoichiometric ratio from our balanced reaction is below : $\text { Stoichiometric ratio }=\frac{1 \text { moles of } \mathrm{N}_2}{4 \text { moles of } \mathrm{HCl}}$ $=\frac{0.25 \text { moles } \mathrm{MnO}_2}{1 \text { moles } \mathrm{HCl}}$ This means we need at least 0.25 mole of $\mathrm{MnO}_2$ for every mole of $\mathrm{HCl}$. Since, our actual ratio is greater then our stoichiometric ratio, we have more $\mathrm{MnO}_2$ than we need to react with each mole of $\mathrm{HCl}$. Therefore, $\mathrm{HCl}$ is our limiting reagent and $\mathrm{MnO}_2$ is in excess. Hence, correct option is (b).
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: 1 g of a complex <math> <mrow> <msub> <mrow> <mtext>[Cr(H</mtext></mrow> <mtext>2</mtext> </msub> <msub> <mrow> <mtext>O)</mtext></mrow> <mtext>5</mtext> </msub> <msub> <mrow> <mtext>Cl]Cl</mtext></mrow> <mtext>2</mtext> </msub> <mo>⋅</mo><msub> <mtext>H</mtext> <mtext>2</mtext> </msub> <mtext>O</mtext></mrow> </math> (mol. Wt. 266.5) was passed through a cation exchanger to produce <math> <mrow> <mtext>HCl</mtext></mrow> </math>. The acid liberated was diluted to 1 litre. The normality of this acid solution is
questions.solution:
Mole of complex =Wmol.wt.=1266.5
1 mole of complex produces 2 mole HCl
[Cr(H 2 O) 5 Cl]Cl 2 → Cr(H 2 O)Cl] 2+ 2Cl –
Mole of HCl formed =1266.5×2
N HCl  =  n V  × n−factor =  2 266.5×1
= 7.5 ×  10 –3  N
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The emperical formula of a compound is $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{O}$ and its vapour density is 45 . What is the molecular formula of the compound?
questions.solution:
Given, empirical formula of compound $=\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{O}$
Vapour density $=45$
Molecular formula $=$ ?
Empirical formulas show the simplest whole number ratio of atoms in a compound.
Molecular formula show the number of each type of atom in a molecule.
Relation between empirical formula and molecular formula.
Molecular formula $=n$ (empirical formula $)$
$n=\frac{\text { MM (molar mass) }}{\text { EFM (empirical formula molar mass) }}$
Molecular weight $=2 \times$ vapour density $=2 \times 45=90$
Let, the molecular forumula be $\mathrm{C}_{2 x} \mathrm{H}_{5 x} \mathrm{O}_x$.
Therefore, $n=\frac{90 \text { (molecular weight })}{47(\text { EFM })}=1.47 \approx 2$
Value of $x=2$
Molecular formula $=\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{O}\right)_2=\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_{10} \mathrm{O}_2$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
